    Galaxy Groups

    Galaxy Groups

    Type:Environment / Structure
    Level:Intermediate
    Membership:~3–50 galaxies
    Mass range:1012–1014 M☉
    Related: Galaxy mergers
    Quenching
    Ram-pressure stripping
    Galaxy clusters

    Galaxy groups are gravitationally bound associations of typically 3–50 galaxies, intermediate in mass between isolated galaxies and massive galaxy clusters. Groups are the most common galaxy environment in the local universe — the majority of galaxies reside in groups rather than clusters or the field[1]. Despite their lower masses and velocity dispersions compared to clusters, groups host significant environmental processing through tidal interactions, mergers, and mild hydrodynamic effects[2].

    Physical Properties

    Galaxy groups have total masses of ~1012–1014 M☉, velocity dispersions of ~100–500 km/s, and virial radii of ~0.5–2 Mpc[1]. Their lower velocity dispersions compared to clusters mean that galaxy–galaxy interactions proceed more slowly, making tidal interactions and mergers more effective than in clusters where high-speed encounters produce weaker tidal perturbations[2].

    Environmental Effects on Galaxy Evolution

    Groups are key sites for galaxy transformation. The environmental dependence of galaxy properties — including star formation rates, stellar ages, and morphology — extends down to group-mass scales[3]. Even in low-mass groups, interactions can flatten age and metallicity gradients, enhance dust content, and trigger quenching in some members[4].

    A striking demonstration comes from comparing the M51 and M101 groups: the more dynamically evolved M51 group shows flattened age–Σ★ and nebular metallicity–Σ★ relations, while the less evolved M101 group preserves steep gradients consistent with undisturbed inside-out formation[4]. The M51a/b interacting pair further illustrates how group interactions produce divergent outcomes — a star-forming spiral alongside a retired early-type companion[4].

    Quenching in Groups

    Galaxy groups contribute significantly to the overall quenching budget in the universe. Mechanisms include tidal interactions that strip or heat gas, strangulation (removal of the hot gas halo that feeds future star formation), and in denser groups, mild ram-pressure stripping[2][3]. M63's outside-in quenching pattern in the M51 group[4] and the fully retired status of M51b[4] demonstrate that these mechanisms operate even in modest group environments.

    See Also

    • Galaxy mergers
    • Star formation quenching
    • Ram-pressure stripping
    • Coma Cluster — massive cluster environment
    • Thainá-Batista et al. (2026) — M51 vs M101 group comparison

    References
    1. Eke, V. R. et al. (2004). "Galaxy groups in the 2dFGRS: the group-finding algorithm and the group catalogue." MNRAS, 348, 866. DOI:10.1111/j.1365-2966.2004.07379.x
    2. Boselli, A. & Gavazzi, G. (2006). "Environmental effects on late-type galaxies in nearby clusters." PASP, 118, 517.
    3. Kauffmann, G. et al. (2004). "Environmental dependence of stellar mass, structure, SF and nuclear activity." MNRAS, 353, 713. DOI:10.1111/j.1365-2966.2004.08117.x
    4. Thainá-Batista, J. et al. (2026). "Spatially resolved stellar populations in the M51 group with J-PLUS. II." A&A (accepted). arXiv:2603.15869
